Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of drift through the week and then realize I had stuff due soon. Most of my studying happened late at night when I couldn't sleep or when I suddenly felt guilty about falling behind. I tried to listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, but I can't say I was super organized about any of it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Sometimes I wouldn't touch anything for a week, then I'd have a burst where I'd try to catch up on multiple courses at once. Deadlines were usually what forced me to actually sit down and do something. I wanted to be more consistent, but life kept getting in the way and honestly, I just couldn't find the motivation most days.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which I often had. Occasionally I'd go on because I was bored and figured I should at least try to do something productive, but those sessions rarely lasted long.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became automatic for me like checking my phone or whatever. There were maybe a couple weeks early in the semester where I tried to make it a habit, but that fell apart pretty quickly. The LMS just felt like another thing demanding my attention that I didn't have the energy for.
Q5: The lecture recordings were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them in the background or relisten to parts I didn't catch the first time. Some of the video content was decent too when I actually watched it. The discussion boards didn't do much for me though.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and the supplementary materials. PDFs just sit there and I never get around to them. The discussion posts felt pointless to me, like I didn't really know what to say and nobody seemed to care anyway. I only did two posts the whole semester because it felt forced.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to passing the exam or an assignment, I'd do it. Otherwise, it felt like busy work. I needed to feel like my time was actually going to pay off somehow. With limited energy and resources, I couldn't afford to waste time on things that seemed optional or unclear.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. I had enough trouble keeping up with the mandatory things. Required activities at least had a deadline attached, which gave me some structure even if I often missed it anyway.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a bunch of audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I didn't have good internet. I'd also ask classmates to explain things to me verbally because that sticks better for me. A lot of my actual learning happened through conversations and listening, not clicking around the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour talking through concepts with a friend or listening to downloaded content while commuting. None of that shows up anywhere. I'd also sometimes just think through problems in my head while doing other stuff. The LMS only sees clicks, not what's actually going on in my brain.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download the audio or video once and then never go back to the LMS for it. My internet isn't always reliable, so offline access was kind of necessary. The system probably thought I watched something for ten minutes when really I listened to it three times over the next week.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I was scared about falling too far behind and that fear kind of paralyzed me instead of motivating me. Money stress and just general life stuff made it hard to focus on coursework. The mindfulness stuff they offered actually helped me calm down a bit.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS. Confusion about what was actually expected also made me avoid things. If I didn't understand an assignment, I'd put it off until it was almost too late, then scramble.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open up videos or readings and just zone out completely. I'd be technically watching something but thinking about a hundred other things. My mind would wander and then I'd realize I had no idea what I just saw. That happened more than I'd like to admit.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd convince myself I'd do them later, and then later never came until the deadline was breathing down my neck. Fear of doing poorly actually made me avoid starting things. It's weird, but the anxiety made me freeze up instead of act.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff until the last possible moment and then rush to submit whatever I could. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy but it was the only system that kind of worked for me.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did make me think about engaging more. But feedback was often delayed or generic, so it didn't always feel worth waiting for. Good feedback made me want to try harder next time. Bad or no feedback made me feel like nobody was really paying attention anyway.
Q18: The course structure was okay but having 12 online courses meant I was spread way too thin. I couldn't give proper attention to any single course. The structure of this particular course was pretty standard, but I needed more flexibility or clearer priorities to actually keep up.
Q19: Having audio and video options helped me a lot since that's how I learn best. But the sheer amount of content across all my courses made it overwhelming. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus extra would have helped. Sometimes I didn't know where to focus my limited time.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happened to me sometimes. I'd log in, click around, watch some stuff, but then not have the energy or clarity to actually complete assignments. Being active doesn't mean being productive. Sometimes you're just treading water trying to figure out what you're supposed to do.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me because I did a lot of learning offline. If someone's good at absorbing material outside the system and can perform on exams, their LMS numbers won't reflect their actual understanding. I scraped by on exams partly because I studied in ways the system couldn't see.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and for how long, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss offline studying, conversations with peers, and whether you were mentally present. A log might say I watched a video, but it can't tell you I was scared and distracted the whole time.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real effort, not just going through the motions. It's when the material sticks and you want to understand it, not just pass. For me, engagement was rare this semester, but when it happened, it was usually through listening and discussing, not clicking.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the semester or how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope. It wouldn't capture the conversations with classmates that helped me understand things, or the times I listened to lectures while walking to clear my head. My real engagement was mostly invisible to the system.
